2026-04-30.articleHtml:
5 Hints for Today's Wordle

  • Start with a word that tests common consonants. Today's answer has a hard ending sound that's appeared in recent puzzles.
  • Only one vowel appears in this word. If you've eliminated A, E, I, or U, you're getting warmer.
  • There's a repeated letter in this puzzle. Look for a consonant that shows up twice.
  • The word begins with C. That's a good place to start narrowing down possibilities.
  • Think of something you might break. This word fits naturally in that context.

Today's Wordle Answer Revealed

The answer to Wordle puzzle #2594 on April 30, 2026 is CROCK. Congratulations to everyone who solved it today!

Why This Word Fits Today's Puzzle

Looking at the letter distribution in today's puzzle, CROCK checks all the boxes. The word starts with C, which is the third most common starting letter in English after S and B. The repeated C gives you that double-letter opportunity that's been showing up frequently in recent puzzles. With only one vowel (the O), this puzzle rewards players who have been systematically eliminating vowels from their guesses.

The ending K creates that hard consonant sound that's become familiar in recent Wordle answers. QUACK appeared just two days ago, and both words end with that distinctive CK combination. This kind of phonetic pattern often shows up in Wordle because the game tends to favor words with recognizable, pronounceable endings.

The structure C-O-C-K follows a pattern where the first and third letters are identical. This is relatively rare in five-letter words but not unheard of. Words like ALLAY, ANNEX, and EGGER share similar structures. When you spot that repeated letter pattern, it can be a strong signal that you're on the right track.

Meaning and Everyday Use

CROCK has a couple of common meanings that most English speakers encounter regularly. The first and most familiar is a pot or jar, typically made of earthenware or ceramic. When someone says "a crock of something," they're usually referring to a large container used for storage, often in kitchens or cellars. Grandmothers and home cooks have used crocks for generations to store pickles, sauerkraut, or kimchi.

The second common usage is as slang meaning nonsense or something worthless. When someone says "that's a crock," they mean it's ridiculous or not worth your time. This meaning comes from the idea of something being "crock," an old word for a worn-out horse, which evolved into meaning anything broken down or useless.

You'll also hear phrases like "crock of shit" or the cleaner "crock" used to dismiss something as untrue or overhyped. Politicians, salespeople, and internet commenters all contribute to keeping this word in regular rotation.

In pottery and ceramics, a crock remains a practical item. Large crocks are still sold for home fermentation projects, and the word shows up in cooking shows, recipe blogs, and kitchen supply catalogs. It's not the most common everyday word, but it has enough presence in American English that most players will recognize it.

How to Work Toward the Answer

When approaching today's puzzle, start by testing common consonants early. Words like CRANE, CHORD, or CORAL give you good coverage of the C and R positions while testing multiple vowels. Since we know the answer starts with C, you can skip straight to testing what comes after that opening.

The single vowel constraint is your biggest friend here. If your first couple of guesses eliminate A, E, I, and U, you're left with O as the only remaining common vowel. That immediately narrows your search space significantly. Words with only O as a vowel are relatively uncommon, which means your options are more limited than you might think.

For the repeated letter, try words that use common consonants twice. BROWN, GREET, or SASSY can help you test whether the double-letter pattern applies. If you get multiple positions of the same letter, you're making progress. The C in CROCK appears at positions one and three, so any word with C in those spots and a different letter in the middle gets you closer.

The ending K should push you toward words with that hard consonant sound. Once you've confirmed the K at position five, your remaining options shrink considerably. CK is a common digraph that appears at the end of many five-letter words, but combined with the C-start constraint, you've got a very short list to work through.

Best Starter Ideas for This Puzzle

For today's puzzle, starters that test the C opening and common vowel positions work well. CRANE is a classic choice that gives you C, R, A, N, and E. If you see yellows on C and E, you know you're getting closer to the answer structure.

CHORD is another solid option because it tests the CH combination and puts O in position three. Since we know the answer has O as its only vowel, seeing that O light up in yellow tells you something important about the word structure.

CORAL works similarly, testing O and A while confirming the C start. If you get multiple consonants right but the vowels don't match, you know to focus on other vowel options.

For players who want to test the double-letter theory directly, words like COUCH or COURT can help. These put C in position one and test whether the double-C pattern might apply.

The key is to get information on the C start and the vowel situation as quickly as possible. Once you confirm the C and narrow down the vowel to O, you've got maybe a dozen realistic options left to work through in your remaining guesses.

Recent Wordle Pattern Notes

Looking at the last five Wordle answers, some interesting patterns emerge. CROCK follows RURAL, which had its own repeated letter (the R). Before that, QUACK ended with that same CK combination we're seeing today. EERIE brought three vowels to the table, and GLOSS featured a double S at the end.

The pattern of repeated letters has been strong recently. Four of the last five answers have featured a letter appearing twice. This suggests the editor has been leaning into double-letter puzzles, which can trip up players who assume each letter in the answer is unique.

Single-vowel answers have also been appearing with some regularity. GLOSS had one vowel, and now CROCK has one vowel. When you see two single-vowel answers in a row, it's worth noting that the average Wordle answer has about 1.5 vowels, so this represents a notable deviation from the norm.

The CK ending appearing in both QUACK and CROCK is particularly interesting. These two answers are consecutive in the sense that they've appeared close together, but they're separated by RURAL and EERIE. This kind of phonetic similarity shows up periodically in Wordle, where the game seems to favor words with familiar sounds and endings.

For tomorrow's puzzle, consider whether the double-letter trend will continue or if we'll see a return to unique-letter answers. Also watch for whether the vowel count goes back up toward the average, or if the single-vowel streak extends to three.

Extra Tips for Keeping a Streak Alive

Streaks are won and lost on the close calls, the puzzles where you're down to your last guess with two possibilities remaining. Here's how to improve your odds on those tense moments.

First, use process of elimination on the remaining letters. If you've got two words that both fit your current information, pick the one that tests letters you know least about. The goal is to guarantee a win on the next guess, not just have a 50-50 shot.

Second, trust the information you have. If your yellows and greens are telling you something specific about letter positions, don't ignore that to chase a long-shot possibility. The math usually favors the answer that fits the data.

Third, consider the keyboard layout. If you've got a letter that appears twice in the answer and you know it's in position one and three, think about which other positions that letter could occupy if you were wrong about the pattern. Sometimes the keyboard can help you eliminate options.

Fourth, when you're truly stuck, go back to basics. Review which letters are most common in English and work through them systematically. Sometimes a fresh start with a common word like ADIEU or AUDIO can reveal information you missed in your earlier guesses.

Fifth, take a break if you need it. Wordle isn't going anywhere, and a two-minute pause to stretch and reset can help you see the puzzle more clearly. Frustration leads to mistakes, and mistakes break streaks.

Frequently Asked Questions

What was the Wordle answer for April 30, 2026?

The answer to Wordle #2594 on April 30, 2026 was CROCK. This five-letter word starts with C, ends with K, and contains a repeated C in the middle position.

How many vowels does today's Wordle answer have?

CROCK has exactly one vowel: the letter O. This makes it a relatively rare puzzle since most Wordle answers contain two vowels. Players who eliminated A, E, I, and U quickly had a significant advantage.

Are repeated letters common in Wordle?

Repeated letters appear in roughly 20-25% of Wordle answers, though recent puzzles have seen an uptick in this pattern. Four of the last five answers have featured repeated letters, which is above the historical average. If you're not considering double-letter possibilities, you might be missing solutions.

What's the best starting word for today's puzzle?

Words that test common consonants and multiple vowels work well as starters. CRANE, CHORD, and CORAL are all solid choices because they test the C opening while giving you information about common vowels. Since the answer starts with C, you can skip the typical starting word advice and go straight to testing what follows that opening consonant.

How does today's puzzle compare to recent Wordle answers?

Today's answer CROCK shares the CK ending with QUACK from two days ago. The recent streak has featured several single-vowel answers and multiple instances of repeated letters. This suggests the puzzle editor has been favoring these patterns, which players who track recent answers could have anticipated.

You'll find this ending combination in several recent Wordle answers.
The repeated letter appears at positions one and three.
Only one vowel appears in this word, making elimination easier.
This word describes something that might sit in your kitchen or garden.
